title: Hello & the Alphabet Overview
subtitle: Listen to the lesson explained in English

transcript.en: Welcome! 👋 Unit 1. Your very first steps into Maltese.

Five big things in this unit. **Meeting people**. **Key phrases** for introducing yourself. **The days of the week**. The **alphabet** with its unique Maltese letters. And the rule for saying **'the'**. Let's dive in!
Imagine you've just moved to Malta. A neighbour knocks. Here's how the conversation goes. Morning greeting:
Welcome! Lovely word. Then comes the big one — nice to meet you:
It means 'Nice to meet you'. Always use the full form — never shorten it. 🤝
To ask where someone is from:
And to answer — I'm from England, but now I live in Malta:

transcript.mt: Jien Ingliża, imma issa ngħix Malta.

transcript.en: Notice the word for 'I', and the verb meaning 'I live'. Compact and confident! 💪
The phrases you'll use from day one. To say hello:
To tell someone your name — my name is:
What do you do for work?
I work as an architect, for example:
How long have you been here?
One more: I'm Maltese — two forms, one for men, one for women:
And if you're an expat — I'm a foreigner:
Always give both the masculine and feminine forms — Maltese marks gender in adjectives and nationalities. 🌍
Seven days, seven chances to practise the article. Listen and watch how 'the' changes each time. 🎧
Notice the article jumping between Il-, It-, L-, Is-. That's the article rule in full swing — and Part 4 explains exactly why. 🌟
Quick fact: Maltese has **30 letters**. Most look like English. But **five are unique**. Let's meet the special ones.
Sounds like the **'j'** in 'judge'. As in:
A strong 'h' — push the air through. As in:
And finally the digraph — two letters, one sound:
Mostly silent — it stretches the vowel next to it. Important: always write the full ligature, never the plain two-letter version. ✨
Maltese is **mostly phonetic**. Once you know the sounds, you can read almost anything. That's a superpower English learners rarely get. 🎯
There's also one vowel you won't find in English — **ie**, written with two letters but counting as one. As in:
This is where Maltese gets interesting. There is **one word for 'the'**, but it reshapes depending on the next sound. Three tidy groups.
GROUP 2 — vowels, h, or għ: drop the 'i', 'the' becomes:
GROUP 3 — the **'Xemxin' consonants**: these nine letters copy the article sound, doubling up. You'll find the full list in the lesson cards. 🌞
There's one special exception: **sk-, sp-, st-** clusters take **l-** plus a helping **i**. So 'the school' is:
Never the doubled form. That cluster just doesn't follow the doubling rule.
Don't try to memorise all the rules at once. The exercises drill them in the easy way. Your ears learn faster than your brain. 👂
Greetings and introductions. The days of the week. 30 letters including five unique ones. And three forms of 'the'. That's Unit 1.
Tap whichever section calls to you first and let the cards do the work. You've already started — that's the hardest step. 💪
